video_title: Market Headwinds vs Tailwinds Make This A Time For Caution | Michael Lebowitz
gist: Portfolio manager Michael Lebowitz argues investors should turn cautious and take chips off the table for the next six months: a wave of mega-IPOs and secondary offerings (SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI, Stripe, Google's $80B) is draining finite liquidity from an already speculative, high-valuation market, while the AI capex boom faces self-cannibalization risk and the bond market hinges almost entirely on whether oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

themes.theme: Liquidity drain from the IPO/secondary supply wave

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO and the pipeline of mega-offerings are a net headwind because they pull a fixed pool of liquidity out of existing equities rather than being absorbed by expanding liquidity as in 2021.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Treat the offering calendar as a liquidity sink; expect persistent pressure on the most-owned large caps as funds raise cash to participate.
insights.quote: if SpaceX is going to put a marble into that jar, one and the jar is full, one of two things has to happen. either the jar has to get bigger to accommodate it or the size of one or all the other marbles has to shrink by a little.

insights.claim: The Mag 7 traded poorly all week because investors pre-sold Nvidia, Apple, and Microsoft to raise cash for SpaceX, treating SpaceX as a like-for-like Mag-7 substitute rather than selling small caps or utilities.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Mega-cap tech may underperform other equity buckets around large IPO/secondary pricing windows as funding sales rotate out of the closest substitutes.
insights.quote: Let me start by making room. I'll sell Nvidia. I'll sell Apple. I'll sell Microsoft. whatever it is.

insights.claim: SpaceX is raising only ~$75B but is expected to land near a ~$2 trillion market cap, and the broader 2026 calendar adds Anthropic, OpenAI, Stripe plus secondaries from Google ($80B), Meta, Microsoft and Amazon.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: A heavy supply calendar all year caps upside; watch issuance dates as potential local tops in mega-cap tech.
insights.quote: markets are all about supply and demand, and there's a lot of new supply coming.

insights.claim: The rotation of speculative money into semis and SpaceX is draining yesterday's speculative darlings, with Bitcoin, gold and silver, GameStop and AMC all following the same decay pattern as money chases the newest thing.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Falling Bitcoin/gold may reflect speculative capital migration rather than fundamentals; rotation timing, not asset thesis, drives these moves.
insights.quote: If the fundament are kind of like yesterday's speculative darling, those are like last years, right?

themes.theme: AI capex boom and its self-cannibalization risk

insights.claim: Hyperscalers other than Nvidia have gone from huge cash flows to flat-to-negative free cash flow, forcing them to fund AI capex through new debt and equity rather than profits.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Funding-driven capex makes hyperscaler balance sheets and dilution a new risk variable; their issuance itself is a market liquidity drain.
insights.quote: Almost all of them, not counting Nvidia, but all the hyperscalers are now flat to negative cash flow, meaning they have they're if they want to keep spending the way they are, they need to borrow money.

insights.claim: Google chose a $80B equity secondary over cheap debt because borrowing creates an asset-liability mismatch (interest due now, AI revenues not arriving until years 3-7) and because its highly valued stock is effectively free, dilutive financing.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: When a company sells stock instead of issuing cheap debt, read it as a signal management views its equity as above intrinsic value.
insights.quote: When your stock is above its intrinsic value and there's no no definitive answer as to what what intrinsic value is, you should be selling stock, not buying stock

insights.claim: The biggest risk to the AI capex trade is AI-driven innovation itself rendering today's massive data centers obsolete, the way room-sized supercomputers were collapsed into far cheaper, more efficient machines.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: An efficiency breakthrough (cheaper compute or leaner software, China-style) could strand hundreds of billions of data-center spend; watch compute-cost vs benefit.
insights.quote: What's going to happen if there's some innovation that and AI can help us with that innovation that turns data centers into the size of this room?

insights.claim: AI is a winner-take-most network-effect market like search: as models commoditize and prices get competed down, most providers won't be able to sustain a business and will drop out, leaving two or three winners.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Frame AI investing like search: identify likely winners now; assume heavy losers among the hyperscalers all chasing the same prize.
insights.quote: it's just there's going to be one big winner and then everybody else because it's uh price is going to be driven down

insights.claim: User-level switching costs are the durable moat in AI: once a model learns a user (premium Claude memory, accumulated context), the friction of retraining a competitor locks the customer in, analogous to Apple Music vs Spotify.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: The race to onboard users early matters more than current model quality; early-mover lock-in is the value driver to track.
insights.quote: the more it learns from you, the more the harder it is for you to switch to another word another one.

insights.claim: Apple has deliberately under-invested in AI and is lying in wait to become the leading consumer/business broker of AI products once the technology and use cases mature in two to five years.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Apple's low AI capex may be a feature not a flaw; potential late-mover beneficiary that avoids the malinvestment risk.
insights.quote: Apple's never the first. Apple doesn't invent anything. They just make everything so much better

insights.claim: An AI bust (a big disappointment) is baked in because real-world physical constraints (property, permits, skilled labor, copper wire) cannot build data centers as fast as the capex projections assume, even when the money and chips are available.
insights.speaker: Jesse Felder (relayed by Adam Taggart)
insights.so_what: If buildout lags projections, analysts must cut earnings estimates and AI-leveraged stocks reprice down; watch construction-pace constraints, not chip supply.
insights.quote: the money is there to be spent. Just the physical things and physical people aren't there to build as fast as the projections are now currently expecting on paper.

insights.claim: Because earnings expectations have accelerated on the assumption of ~$1T AI capex this year rising thereafter, any shortfall in actual AI spending would force earnings downgrades and cascade into broad market and economic weakness.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: AI capex is the load-bearing input for index earnings; a spending miss is the single most likely domino to start a broad selloff.
insights.quote: if something were to make those spending flows less than is currently being expected that could really have a big impact on the system

insights.claim: Adam coins the 'Maleficent 7' scenario: if AI buildout hits Felder's constraints, the Magnificent 7 could flip from pulling the market up to dragging it down year after year.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Concentration cuts both ways; the same names driving gains could amplify a drawdown if AI disappoints.
insights.quote: the Magnificent 7 could metastasize into the Maleficent 7

themes.theme: Oil, the Strait of Hormuz, and the bond market

insights.claim: The bond market has become a hostage to oil: yields will fall when oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz regardless of any signed Iran peace deal, easing inflation and supporting stocks.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Trade the commodity, not the headlines; oil through the strait is the catalyst for lower rates and an equity tailwind.
insights.quote: Bonds are bonds have become a hostage to the price

insights.claim: Five-year Treasury yields are irrationally elevated because the market is overweighting near-term oil-driven inflation, while 5-year inflation expectations have barely risen.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Potential long-duration opportunity if transitory oil inflation rolls off and yields normalize toward stable 5-year expectations.
insights.quote: I think they're irrationally pricing in a lot of this inflation that it is temporary.

insights.claim: Oil futures are already pricing a lower price later in 2026 than the front contract, signaling the market expects the Strait of Hormuz to reopen soon, with Brent and WTI in the mid-80s.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: The futures curve is the cleanest read on the geopolitics; backwardation here corroborates a near-term de-escalation base case.
insights.quote: oil seems to be pricing in that the straight of hormuz is going to be opening soon

insights.claim: There is a contrarian risk that oil stays elevated far longer than expected even if the strait reopens, because global inventories are critically low (Jeff Curry) and getting the right oil grades to the right places is a persistent logistics problem (Art Berman).
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: If oil stays high, the lower-rates tailwind reverses into a wrench; hedge the bullish-bond thesis against a sticky-oil scenario.
insights.quote: there's a real danger of even if the straight opens tomorrow that we have elevated oil prices for a lot longer than the market is currently expecting.

insights.claim: The base case for de-escalation is the political incentive: no incumbent can hold the House or Senate in November with gasoline at $4-6/gallon, so the administration will do whatever it takes to push oil into the $2-3 range.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Election arithmetic supports lower oil into the midterms; the main downside is Iran refusing to play ball.
insights.quote: Do whatever he can to get oil back down to the twos or three.

themes.theme: Speculative regime, valuations and the case for caution

insights.claim: Markets have been in a rolling-bubble speculative regime since 2020, with 0DTE options now over half of volume, leveraged ETFs proliferating (including 10 SpaceX leveraged ETFs before it even trades), and sequential manias in memes, crypto, even utilities.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Position for high volatility and fast rotations; the speculative impulse will chase the next thing rather than sustain any one trade.
insights.quote: zero uh data exper options they are accounting for over half the volume those are roulette binary

insights.claim: Lebowitz's overarching call is to be cautious and take a few chips off the table for the next six months, expecting elevated volatility and a market that may round-trip to where it started the year.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Bias to defense: trim risk into strength; downside skew outweighs the marginal upside given valuations and the supply overhang.
insights.quote: if you want to take a few chips off the table for the next six months

insights.claim: Mega-IPO clusters historically signal a late-stage bull cycle as insiders monetize while buyers will absorb anything, validated by Carlota Perez's framework that early innovation leaders rarely become the eventual winners.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Don't assume today's AI leaders are permanent; treat the IPO surge as a late-cycle tell and stay selective.
insights.quote: the initial leaders in AI, the initial leaders in the internet... aren't the ones that are the companies today.

insights.claim: The economy is bifurcated: consumer weakness (record-low savings rate, rising credit-card delinquencies, soft retail) is being masked by AI spending, while GDP and manufacturing remain solid in a K-shaped split.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Headline strength is propped by AI capex; if that fades, masked consumer weakness surfaces fast. Watch delinquencies and retailer commentary.
insights.quote: consumer weakness is being masked by AI spending

insights.claim: A divided Congress after the midterms is likely market-positive because markets prize the certainty of gridlock over any partisan outcome, though heightened political sensitivity raises volatility and shutdown risk.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: A Democratic House win may be a non-event or mild positive for stocks via gridlock; size political volatility as a separate risk.
insights.quote: basically it means gridlock and you know what markets they don't necessarily... have a partisan preference as much as they just think that they just like certainty

themes.theme: Quantum computing as encryption and asset risk

insights.claim: If quantum computing breaks encryption as advertised, Bitcoin's value approaches zero because its security is just a code a quantum machine could brute-force, unlike a physical gold bar.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Quantum progress is a tail risk for crypto and a relative bull case for physical, un-hackable assets like gold; track quantum milestones.
insights.quote: if quantum computing is what they say it was, encryption is meaningless... If encryption is meaningless, Bitcoin is it has zero value.

insights.claim: Google and IBM expect commercially relevant quantum products by 2029-2030, and the investable space spans eight or nine different qubit approaches with large diversified players (Google, IBM) and highly volatile pure-play smaller companies.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: It's a 'wild west' with no settled protocol winner; expect big winners and losers among pure-plays and extreme volatility if you participate.
insights.quote: they think they will have relevant products out there by 2029, 2030.

insights.claim: Investors should size portfolios to the specific rate of return their financial plan requires (e.g. 5%), not to beating the index, taking the least risk needed rather than maximizing return.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz / Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Adopt a 'golf' (lowest risk for the needed score) rather than 'basketball' (highest score) mindset; you can lock ~5% in a 30-year Treasury fund without index risk.
insights.quote: What I care about is where's how is my wealth growing to meet my needs?

insights.claim: As an investor makes progress toward goals, the required return curve flattens (12% to 9% to 6%), so the right question each year is whether you can take less risk while still hitting the plan.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: De-risk progressively; success should buy certainty, not push you to keep reaching for index-level returns.
insights.quote: as you get more and more successful, doesn't that that needed return curve start to to bend more level?

themes.theme: Wealth concentration, fiscal priorities and social tensions

insights.claim: SpaceX began trading near $163 (up ~20% from a ~$135 price), making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire and intensifying record wealth inequality in an 'i-shaped' economy with a tiny advantaged few atop a large left-behind base.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart / Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Extreme wealth concentration compounds via political and reinvestment advantage, a structural driver of social and policy instability investors must price.
insights.quote: up 20% for the day from when it was first um from the what $135 price.

insights.claim: Lebowitz argues for forcing corporate behavior via higher taxes that compel innovation spending or pay the tax, framing innovation as a societal benefit worth distorting libertarian instincts to encourage.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Signals a possible policy direction (higher corporate/estate taxation, redistribution) that could reshape capital allocation if the fourth-turning social pressure builds.
insights.quote: How about raising corporate taxes? Force them to spend the money on innovation and if they can't spend it, pay higher taxes on it.

insights.claim: A widening K-shaped divide and a sense among citizens that resources flow to immigrants and corporations rather than them is building toward a social boiling-over point, with parallels already visible in the UK and Ireland.
insights.speaker: Adam Taggart
insights.so_what: Rising social instability is a slow-burn macro risk that could drive disruptive redistributive or democratic-socialist policy shifts.
insights.quote: I think there is a social boiling over point in there

insights.claim: Even the three poorest US states (Mississippi, Arkansas and one other) have GDP per capita roughly equal to or higher than European countries, Canada and Japan, underscoring relative US productivity.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Frames the inequality debate: US prosperity is high in absolute and relative terms, which conditions how redistribution arguments and capital flows play out.
insights.quote: Their GDP per capita is virtually the same or higher than all the European countries, Canada, you name it, Japan

insights.claim: RIA made no trades this week after a mild growth-to-value shift two to three weeks prior: taking profits in tech names and buying value names including Walmart, Costco and Coca-Cola.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Mirrors the cautious thesis in practice: rotating from growth into defensive value while standing pat through the IPO volatility window.
insights.quote: we took some profits in a lot of our tech names. We were sh we were doing a mild shift from growth to value.

insights.claim: Dealers are heavily incentivized to keep the market calm and ensure SpaceX trades well through early next week to prove they can handle the coming IPO and secondary pipeline.
insights.speaker: Michael Lebowitz
insights.so_what: Expect engineered calm/support near big offerings; the more honest volatility read may come after the dealer incentive fades (~mid next week).
insights.quote: the dealers are incentivized to just keep calm. And I think we'll still see that through that through early next week.
